content:
2 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Many people believe that as AI becomes better at understanding human behavior,
it may eventually become more similar to the way humans think and understand the world.
Recently, however, I’ve been thinking about a different question:
As AI learns more about us,
are we also learning more about ourselves?
Sometimes,
when I discuss an idea with AI,
the most valuable part isn’t the answer.
It’s what I discover about my own thinking.
I start to notice how I approach a problem.
What I pay attention to.
What matters most to me.
And sometimes,
I realize that the question I thought was important
isn’t actually the question I wanted to ask.
Through these conversations,
AI can feel a bit like a mirror.
It doesn’t just respond to questions.
Sometimes,
it helps us notice thoughts and assumptions we didn’t realize we had.
It may not tell us who we are.
But it gives us an opportunity to see
how we think,
how we make decisions,
and how we choose our direction.
Perhaps the value of AI is not only that it can understand people better.
Perhaps its greater value is helping people understand themselves better.
Because sometimes,
what truly needs to be seen
isn’t the answer.
It’s the person searching for it.
🤔
